steps.text: Determine whether the scope of experiments conducted at the facility were consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.
Determine whether the licensee’s procedures for the control and conduct of experiments are consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.

Determine whether the licensee reviewed and approved experiments performed at the facility, and any subsequent changes made to the experiments, in accordance with Technical Specification (TS) requirements and the licensee’s procedural requirements.
Determine whether the licensee reviewed experiments for TS changes or compliance to 10 CFR 50.59.

Determine whether the licensee identified hazards associated with the experiments conducted since the last inspection and took appropriate remedial action, in accordance with the licensee’s procedures.
Determine whether the licensee predicted the approximate reactivity effect on the reactor before conducting the experiment.
Determine whether the licensee confirmed the reactivity effect as required by the TS.

Determine whether the licensee accounted for irradiated items until disposing of them or until they decayed to an acceptable level, in accordance with regulatory requirements and applicable licensee procedures.
Determine whether the licensee’s experiments are constrained as
Determine whether the licensee’s experiments are constrained as required by the TS.
Determine whether researchers adhered to the licensees procedural controls when loading experiments into the test facilities and removing them from the test facilities.
Determine whether the experiments reduced the operator’s ability to control reactivity manipulations or the protective system’s ability to shut down the reactor if needed.
Determine whether engineered radiation safety controls were used during experiments and posting was accomplished as required.
Examine new experiments and changes to the licensee’s experiments and related programs since the last inspection.

Compare the list of experiments from the Annual Report or experiment logs to respective license conditions, TS, Safety Analysis Report (SAR), Safety Evaluation Report (SER), and supporting documentation.
Review the available documentation prior to the onsite inspection.
Review the licensee's procedures for the control and conduct of experiments prior to the onsite inspection.
Directly observe 50 percent of the new experiments, or changes to experiments, since the last inspection.
Review records and conduct discussions with cognizant personnel for the sample size.

Review the information available to the operator that authorizes the conduct of a particular experiment to determine whether it has been reviewed and approved in accordance with licensee's TS and procedural requirements.
Insert, disassemble, or remove experiments from the reactor.
Conduct experiments in accordance with the licensee's administrative control procedures.
Monitor and understand the meaning of gauges that measure pressures, including vacuum conditions, where appropriate to the experimental setup.
Verify that the applicable TS limits for reactivity, such as shutdown margin, excess reactivity, and individual and total worth of experiments were not exceeded.
Stop the experiment and calculate the value to assure compliance with constraint limits and shutdown margin limits.
Stop the experiment.
Verify that the recipient is licensed to possess the type, form, and quantity of material to be transferred.
Establish secure arrangements so that a single movement does not pulse a non-pulsing reactor, or exceed TS requirements or safety analysis assumptions.
Insert or remove experiments (samples) with a worth of less than 0.002 delta K/K with a rabbit system or by hand while the reactor is critical.
Fix the experiment in place if its worth is greater than 0.006 delta K/K.
Discuss the methods of handling experiments and examine the process for consistency with the licensee's administrative controls and procedures.
Evaluate how the licensee assures that experiments do not cause reactivity anomalies, considering operator control, detector shadowing, and control rod interference.
Verify if the licensee has considered the use of engineered safety features such as remote handling devices, time, distance, and shielding in the planning and conduct of experiments.
Verify that access to areas where radiation is present is posted in accordance with 10 CFR Parts 20.1902 and 20.1903 and limited as required by the licensee's radiation protection program.

preconditions.text: The facilities and materials being inspected must be licensed under 10 CFR Part 50 and not governed by other types of licenses.
The experiments or changes to experiments have not been previously reviewed by NRC personnel.
The documentation is already available at the office.
It is practical to review the procedures prior to the onsite inspection.
A written description of reactor operations (from insertion until removal) approved by the safety review committee is obtained before an experiment is inserted.
NRC review and approval by the Office of Nuclear Reactor Regulation (NRR) is obtained if the experiment involves the irradiation of explosives.
If a regulating rod is changed by the established conservative cut-off amount
A regulating rod is changed by the amount specified by the licensee.
The expected radiation field for the personnel does not present an exposure problem.
Removal of an experiment from the reactor cannot be directly observed.

constraints.text: Guidance documents (ANSI/ANS Standards, Division 2 Regulatory Guides) are to aid technical evaluation and must not be used as requirements unless the licensee has committed in writing to use them.

Particular attention is to be given to experiments that contain special nuclear material (fueled experiments) or explosives. The failure of such experiments may result in a significant radioactive release and may be the maximum hypothetical accident at some reactors.
Experiments must be inserted, disassembled, or removed under the direct supervision of the reactor supervisor, or a duly authorized SRO.
Operators and experimenters must understand the hazards associated with an experiment and the methods to identify and respond to them.
If the capsule has ruptured, the opening of the experiment tube could be radiologically hazardous or cause a reactivity transient.
Control irradiated articles in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 until final disposal.
Make final disposal in accordance with Subpart K of 10 CFR Part 20.
Transfer byproduct material produced or used under the reactor (10 CFR Part 50) license in accordance with 10 CFR 30.41.
An experiment in a pool-type reactor escaping its constraints and floating to the surface of the pool can cause potential reactivity and exposure problems.

If the experiment has a reactivity worth of between 0.002 and around 0.006 delta K/K, it must be mildly constrained (e.g., by resting the experiment on a support instead of dangling it from a string or wire).
When an experiment is being removed from the reactor, it is assumed to be contaminated and highly radioactive until proven otherwise.
Experiments that are of negative reactivity on insertion into the reactor core are positive on withdrawal and must meet TS requirements.

Apparatus and mechanisms other than controls, the operation of which may affect the reactivity or power level of a reactor, must be manipulated only with the knowledge and consent of a licensed operator or SRO.
Engineered safety controls for experiments must effectively limit occupational exposures to the levels required by 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee's administrative limits.
Planning and conduct of experiments must be consistent with the principle of as low as reasonably achievable (LARA).
